name: Yaqub and Yusuf, part 1
epithet: A dream, a brother's envy, and a father's beautiful patience
title: Yaqub and Yusuf, part 1: the dream, the well, and beautiful patience
meta_description: Day 15 of 29: Yusuf's dream of eleven stars, the sun and the moon, his brothers' envy, the well, the false blood on the shirt, and Yaqub's beautiful patience when his beloved son is taken. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: Yusuf, Yaqub, Joseph, Jacob, Surah Yusuf, the dream eleven stars, the well, sabrun jamil, beautiful patience, Mufti Menk, stories of the prophets, Buruja

video.title: Stories Of The Prophets-15~Yaqub and Yusuf - Part 1

source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 15 (Yaqub and Yusuf, part 1). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Allah kept prophethood in the line of Ibrahim, peace be upon him: through his son Ishaq, then Ishaq's son Yaqub, peace be upon them, the man also called Israil, the father of the twelve tribes. And it is to one of Yaqub's twelve sons that Allah gives something He gives no other prophet: an entire surah of his own, told from beginning to end in one unbroken sweep, and named by Allah Himself the most beautiful of stories.

This is day fifteen of twenty-nine, and the first of three days inside that surah. It opens the way so many tests open: not with a disaster, but with a gift. A boy has a dream. And the people who should have rejoiced for him decide instead to bury him.

ayah.arabic: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَٰفِلِينَ
ayah.text: We relate to you, [O Muḥammad], the best of stories in what We have revealed to you of this Qur’ān although you were, before it, among the unaware.

paras: Mufti Menk begins with the lineage, because it matters. Ibrahim was given Ismail and Ishaq, peace be upon them all, and Ibrahim lived long enough to see his grandson Yaqub born and to speak with him; the Qur'an records Ibrahim advising both Ishaq and Yaqub. Yaqub, peace be upon him, was given twelve sons: ten from one mother, and two, Yusuf and the one named as Binyamin, from another. The whole nation later called Bani Israil, the Children of Israel, are the descendants of those twelve, for Israil was Yaqub's other name. So when the Qur'an speaks of the Children of Israel, it is speaking of this one family, in this one home.

The companions once asked the Prophet ﷺ to tell them a beautiful story, and Allah answered by revealing the surah of Yusuf, telling His Messenger ﷺ plainly that before this revelation he had no knowledge of it. That is the first thing to hold onto. This is not a remembered legend polished by retelling. It is the record of the One who watched it happen, handed down as the most beautiful of stories, and Mufti Menk's whole counsel across these three nights is the same: take the maximum lessons out of it.

sections.h2: A boy, a dream, and a father who understood

ayah.arabic: إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِى سَٰجِدِينَ
ayah.text: [Of these stories mention] when Joseph said to his father, "O my father, indeed I have seen [in a dream] eleven stars and the sun and the moon; I saw them prostrating to me."

paras: Yusuf was the youngest, and one morning he woke up bright with excitement and ran to his father. Not with a nightmare, the way children usually come running, but with a wonder: eleven stars, and the sun, and the moon, and all of them bowing down to him. Yaqub was a prophet, and he understood at once that this was no ordinary dream. A child does not see the heavens prostrate to him for nothing. Allah, through revelation, let Yaqub read it: this little one would be raised high, chosen, given a station above the rest.

And the Qur'an lets us hear Yaqub teach his son what the dream was pointing toward, that Allah would choose him, would teach him the meaning of dreams and events, and would complete His favour on him as He had completed it on Ibrahim and Ishaq before. A father looking at his youngest son and seeing, in a dream of stars, the unfinished line of Ibrahim still being written through this boy.

ayah.arabic: قَالَ يَٰبُنَىَّ لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ فَيَكِيدُوا۟ لَكَ كَيْدًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَٰنَ لِلْإِنسَٰنِ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ
ayah.text: He said, "O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers or they will contrive against you a plan. Indeed Satan, to man, is a manifest enemy."

paras: Here is the first lesson Mufti Menk lingers on, and it is a quiet one. The father's instinct is not pride but protection: do not tell this to your brothers, he says, or they will plot against you. And notice who he names as the enemy. Not the boys. Shaytan. The children are good, the father is saying; it is the whisperer who turns a brother's love into a brother's envy, slipping into the smallest crack until he widens it into a crater. Good news, Mufti Menk draws out, is not for everyone. Some of those closest to you cannot carry it without it curdling. Thank Allah for the blessing, and keep some of it quietly between you and Him.

But Yusuf was a young boy, glad and innocent, and the advice did not land. We do not even know if he understood that his father had forbidden it; children miss the weight of what a parent warns them about, and Mufti Menk is honest that he himself was once such a child. Yusuf told his brothers. A small disobedience, the kind any child commits, and from it the whole storm would blow. The Prophet ﷺ later warned a companion who wished to favour one child with a gift: either give to all of them, or give to none, because uneven hands plant a hatred between children that the parents never see growing.

ayah.arabic: إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ
ayah.text: When they said, "Joseph and his brother are more beloved to our father than we, while we are a clan. Indeed, our father is in clear error.

ayah.arabic: ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَٰلِحِينَ
ayah.text: Kill Joseph or cast him out to [another] land; the countenance [i.e., attention] of your father will [then] be only for you, and you will be after that a righteous people."

paras: The brothers held a quiet meeting of their own. Look how our father loves Yusuf and his brother more than us, they said, and we are the strong ones, the grown men, the clan. They even called their father, a prophet of Allah, plainly mistaken. And here Mufti Menk presses the lesson hardest: this is what happens to a problem you refuse to solve. They could have gone to their father and said it to his face, we feel you favour him, help us understand. Instead they let it sit. Sit, and sour, and swell, until it grew into the unthinkable. Kill Yusuf, one of them said, or throw him into some far-off land, and then your father's attention will be yours alone, and afterwards you can be righteous people.

Mufti Menk will not let us file this under bad children. This was the household of a prophet, a man who raised his sons on the message and was believed by them. If Shaytan could reach that far into that home, none of us should grow comfortable about our own. Solve the matter today, he says, while it is small. Go to the person directly; do not let the story brew behind their back. A grievance carried into tomorrow is heavier than the one you could have set down today.

ayah.arabic: قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَٰعِلِينَ
ayah.text: Said a speaker among them, "Do not kill Joseph but throw him into the bottom of the well; some travelers will pick him up - if you would do [something]."

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ
ayah.text: So when they took him [out] and agreed to put him into the bottom of the well... But We inspired to him, "You will surely inform them [someday] about this affair of theirs while they do not perceive [your identity]."

paras: One brother, the one whose heart still pulled toward Yusuf, talked them down from murder. Do not kill him, he said. Throw him into the bottom of a well instead, and let some passing caravan carry him away. Then the plan needed only their father's permission. So they came to Yaqub all sweetness: why will you not trust us with Yusuf, when we only want the best for him? Send him out with us tomorrow to eat and play, and we will guard him. Yaqub, sharp enough to feel the rivalry beneath the words, told them the truth of his fear: it grieves me that you take him, and I am afraid a wolf will eat him while you are not watching. They had an answer ready, because they always do, Mufti Menk says, do not underestimate what children will produce when cornered: a wolf, eat him, while we are a whole clan? Then we would be the real losers.

Yaqub let him go. And they took him out and lowered him into the dark of the well, after telling him to take off his shirt, as if it were all a game of hide and seek. Their own brother, a child, at the bottom of a pit. But read the verse to its end, because this is the turn that holds the whole surah: in that very moment, Allah inspired Yusuf that he would one day stand before these brothers and tell them exactly what they had done, and they would not even know him. The pit was not the end of Yusuf. It was the first step of the dream. Allah was already steadying the heart of the child at the bottom of the well, and, as the next days will show, the heart of the father at home.

sections.h2: False blood, and a patience that is beautiful

ayah.text: And they came to their father at night, weeping.

ayah.arabic: وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ ۚ قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ
ayah.text: And they brought upon his shirt false blood. [Jacob] said, "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something, so patience is most fitting. And Allāh is the one sought for help against that which you describe."

paras: They came home at night, weeping. Allah uses the word for real tears, Mufti Menk notes, which is why he wonders aloud whether these were the tears of grief or the tears of a sympathy-seeker, the kind a person can summon to be believed. Father, they cried, we went off racing and left Yusuf with our things, and a wolf ate him, the exact wolf their father had named days before, handed straight back to him. And then the proof: the shirt, smeared with blood. But they had cut some animal and sprayed its blood on the cloth and forgotten the one thing the truth would have left behind. The shirt was whole. If a wolf had truly devoured the boy, the shirt would have been torn to pieces. Yaqub saw an unbroken shirt soaked in blood, and he knew.

And here is the line this whole day is built to reach, the words a grieving father chose at the worst moment of his life. He did not rage, and he did not collapse. Your souls, he told them, have talked you into something. So patience, beautiful patience, sabrun jamil. And Allah is the One whose help I seek against what you describe. Patience that does not curdle into bitterness, does not lash out, does not put its complaint on display, only turns, quietly, to Allah. He had just lost his most beloved son to a lie he could see through, and he reached not for revenge but for sabrun jamil. That phrase is Yaqub's gift to every believer who has ever been wronged by the people closest to them. Mufti Menk's reading is steady: a believer schooled by revelation can tell the false from the true, and still choose patience over the satisfaction of being proven right.

ayah.arabic: وَجَآءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا۟ وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ يَٰبُشْرَىٰ هَٰذَا غُلَٰمٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَٰعَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ
ayah.text: And there came a company of travelers; then they sent their water drawer, and he let down his bucket. He said, "Good news! Here is a boy." And they concealed him, [taking him] as merchandise; and Allāh was Knowing of what they did.

ayah.arabic: وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ
ayah.text: And they sold him for a reduced price - a few dirhams - and they were, concerning him, of those content with little.

paras: The scene changes to the well. A caravan passed, and they sent a man ahead to draw water. He let his bucket down into the dark, and when he pulled it up, a boy was clinging to it. Good news, he cried, a boy! And at once they hid him away as merchandise, the way people who deal in the lost and the stolen always do, Mufti Menk says: not how do I return this, but how much can I get for it. They smuggled Yusuf off as goods to be sold, and Allah, the verse ends, knew exactly what they were doing.

And so a prophet's son, a boy a father had wept to part with, was sold in the slave market for a reduced price, a few dirhams you could count on your fingers, by hands content to take next to nothing just to be rid of him. That is where part one leaves Yusuf: at the bottom of a forgotten well a moment ago, and now a slave carried toward Egypt, sold cheap by the very brothers Allah had already promised would one day stand before him not knowing his face. Tomorrow, the house he is bought into, and the test waiting for him there.

dua.en: So patience is most fitting, and Allah is the one sought for help. (the words of Yaqub, Surah Yusuf 12:18)

lessons_intro: Part one of the most beautiful of stories is a chain of small things going wrong, and Mufti Menk draws the lessons out of each link.

lessons.bold: Not every blessing is for sharing.
lessons.text: Yaqub told his son to keep the dream to himself. Good news in the wrong ears can sour into envy. Thank Allah for the gift, and keep some of it quietly between you and Him.
The father did not call the brothers bad. He named Shaytan, who slips into the smallest crack between people and widens it. The problem is rarely the person in front of you.
The brothers let their grievance brew instead of taking it to their father's face. A problem left for tomorrow only grows heavier. Go to the person directly, and clear your heart.
Uneven love plants a hatred parents never see growing. Either give to all of them, the Prophet ﷺ taught, or give to none. Fairness in giving and in correcting both.

Wronged by his own sons, Yaqub did not rage or despair. Sabrun jamil: patience without bitterness, that makes no show of its grief and turns only to Allah. The wound is real; the response is still your choice.

faq.q: Where in the Qur'an is the story of Yusuf?
faq.a: It fills Surah Yusuf, the twelfth surah, told in one unbroken sweep from beginning to end, which no other prophet's story receives. Allah calls it 'the best of stories' (ahsan al-qasas) in 12:3, and tells the Prophet ﷺ that he had no knowledge of it before it was revealed. This entry covers roughly the first twenty verses; the trial in Egypt and the throne come in the days that follow.

faq.q: What was Yusuf's dream, and what did it mean?
faq.a: He saw eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrating to him (12:4). Yaqub, peace be upon him, a prophet, understood by Allah's leave that it foretold his son being raised to a high and chosen station, and told him Allah would choose him and teach him the interpretation of dreams and events (12:6). The eleven stars are his brothers, and the sun and moon his parents, as the surah itself confirms much later when the dream comes true.

faq.q: What does 'sabrun jamil', beautiful patience, mean?
faq.a: It is the patience Yaqub reached for when his son was taken and a bloodied shirt was laid before him: 'so patience is most fitting, and Allah is the one sought for help against what you describe' (12:18). Mufti Menk renders it as a patience that stays beautiful, that does not curdle into bitterness, does not lash out, and makes no public show of its complaint, but turns quietly to Allah alone. It is patience that keeps its dignity.

faq.q: How does this connect to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ?
faq.a: Surah Yusuf was revealed to the Prophet ﷺ during the Year of Sorrow, after he lost his wife Khadijah and his uncle Abu Talib and was driven out of Ta'if: a story of a beloved son betrayed by his own family, who is patient, and whom Allah raises in the end. It came to comfort him ﷺ in his hardest year. The companions had asked for a beautiful story, and Allah answered with a man whose beautiful patience was rewarded, so His Messenger ﷺ and every believer after him would know how the test is passed.

faq.q: Were the brothers evil, if this was a prophet's household?
faq.a: Mufti Menk's whole point is that they were not born monsters. This was the home of Yaqub, peace be upon him, a prophet who raised his sons on the message. The warning is exactly that Shaytan reached even there, through an envy left unspoken and a problem left to brew. If it could happen in that house, no family should grow complacent about its own. Their later remorse, and Yusuf's forgiveness, come in part three.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Part one of the most beautiful of stories does not end beautifully. It ends with a boy at the bottom of a well, then sold cheap in a market, and a father at home holding an unbroken shirt and choosing, against every instinct, beautiful patience. But Allah has already let us hear the secret He whispered into the dark of that well: Yusuf will stand before these brothers again, and they will not know him. The pit is not the end. It is the first line of the dream coming true.

If something has been taken from you by the people who should have protected you, this is your story too, and Yaqub's three words are the gift inside it: sabrun jamil. Patience that keeps its beauty. O Allah, when we are wronged by those closest to us, grant us the beautiful patience of Yaqub, save us from the envy that overtook his sons, and let us trust, as Yusuf trusted at the bottom of the well, that You are working a good ending we cannot yet see. You are the One whose help is sought, and Your help is near. Ameen.

related.label: When life gets in the way
related.note: Yaqub's beautiful patience when everything was taken from him

slides:
Day 15 of 29.

Allah gives Yusuf something He gives no other prophet: a whole surah of his own, named by Allah the most beautiful of stories.

And it opens not with a disaster, but with a gift.

A boy wakes up bright with excitement and runs to his father.

Eleven stars, the sun, and the moon, all bowing down to me.

Yaqub, a prophet, understood at once: this child will be raised high.

Do not tell your brothers, the father said.

Not because the boys are bad. Because Shaytan is.

Good news in the wrong ears can curdle into envy. Some blessings you keep between you and Allah.

The brothers let it brew.

Look how our father loves him more than us. And we are the strong ones.

Mufti Menk: a problem you refuse to solve only grows. Solve it today, to their face, while it is small.

So they lowered their own brother into a well, a child, and told him it was a game.

But read the verse to the end:

Allah inspired Yusuf, in that very pit, that he would stand before them one day and they would not know him.

They came home weeping, with a bloodied shirt.

A wolf ate him, they cried.

But the shirt was whole. If a wolf had really eaten the boy, the shirt would have been torn. Yaqub saw it, and he knew.

And the words a grieving father chose at the worst moment of his life:

Sabrun jamil. Beautiful patience.

Patience that does not curdle into bitterness, makes no show of its grief, and turns only to Allah.

A caravan drew its bucket from the well, and pulled up a boy.

They sold him cheap, a few dirhams you could count on your fingers.

The pit is not the end of Yusuf. It is the first line of the dream coming true.
social.caption: Day 15 of 29: Yaqub and Yusuf, part 1. A boy dreams of eleven stars bowing down. His brothers, eaten by envy, throw him into a well and sell him for a few coins, then carry a bloodied shirt back to their father. And Yaqub, who can see straight through the lie, chooses not revenge but sabrun jamil: beautiful patience, the kind that turns only to Allah. The whole surah was revealed to comfort the Prophet ﷺ in his Year of Sorrow. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (the episode is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day, connected back to the Qur'an and the life of the Prophet ﷺ, at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #Yusuf #SurahYusuf #Quran #Buruja #islam #sabr

email.subject: A boy is thrown into a well by his own brothers
email.alt_subject: Beautiful patience, when everything is taken from you
email.preview: Day 15 of 29: the most beautiful of stories begins, with a dream and a betrayal.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

Today Buruja steps into the one story Allah calls the most beautiful of all, told in a single surah from beginning to end: the story of Yusuf, peace be upon him. This is part one of three.

It begins with a gift. A boy wakes up glowing and runs to his father: I saw eleven stars, and the sun, and the moon, all prostrating to me. His father Yaqub, peace be upon him, a prophet, understood at once that this child was destined to be raised high. And his first instinct was to protect him: do not tell your brothers, he warned, naming not the boys but Shaytan as the danger, the whisperer who slips into the smallest crack between people and widens it.

The boy told them anyway. And the envy the father feared was left to brew, until it became the unthinkable. They lowered their own brother, a child, into the bottom of a well and told him it was a game. They came home weeping with a bloodied shirt, claiming a wolf had eaten him, and forgot the one thing that would have given them away: the shirt was whole. Yaqub saw straight through it.

And here is the line this whole day is built to reach, the words a grieving father chose at the worst moment of his life: 'so patience is most fitting (sabrun jamil), and Allah is the one sought for help against what you describe' (Surah Yusuf 12:18). Beautiful patience. The kind that does not curdle into bitterness, does not lash out, makes no show of its grief, and turns only to Allah. Mufti Menk's reading is steady: a believer can see the lie clearly and still choose patience over the satisfaction of being right.

But read the well-verse to its end and you find the secret Allah whispered into the dark: He inspired Yusuf, in that very pit, that he would stand before these brothers one day, and they would not know him. The pit was not the end. It was the first line of the dream coming true. The whole surah, the scholars note, was revealed to comfort the Prophet ﷺ in his Year of Sorrow, a man betrayed and grieving, shown how the test is passed.

Today Yusuf is sold cheap, for a few dirhams, and carried toward Egypt. Tomorrow, the house he is bought into. Tonight, just Yaqub's words to carry: sabrun jamil, beautiful patience, and Allah is the One whose help we seek.

Read day 15, and watch Mufti Menk's full telling, at the link below.
